content.text: Enhancing Neural Plasticity Through Multi-Modal Interventions in Aging Adults: A Randomized Controlled Trial
Cognitive giaging, nueral plasticity, fMRI, EEG, cognitive trianang, physical exercise, nutritional supplementation
Address: Jl. Merpati No. 24, Kelurahan Menteng, Avendix 10340, Pluto

Age-related cognitive decline is one of the most pressing public health challenges of the 21st century. As populations age globally, the burden of dementia, mild cognitive impairment, and associated healthcare costs continues to rise. Current pharmaceutical interventions have yielded limited success, often targeting symptoms rather than underlying neural mechanisms. In contrast, lifestyle-based, non-pharmacological approaches—such as cognitive training, physical activity, and dietary supplementation—offer promising, low-risk, and sustainable strategies for enhancing neuroplasticity and preserving cognitive function in aging adults.
designed to test the synergistic effects of a

integrating (1) adaptive cognitive training, (2) structured aerobic exercise, and (3) targeted nutritional supplementation. We hypothesize that these three modalittteis, when combined, will yield greater improvements in neural connectivity and cognitive performance than when implemented individually.

Emerging research indicates that cognitive training enhances task-specific neural efficiency (Smith et al., 2022), aerobic exercise promotes angiogenesis and hippocampal volume maintenance (Erickson et al., 2019), and nutritional supplementation—particularly omega-3 fatty acids, B-vitamins, and vitamin D—supports myelination and synaptic repair (Witte et al., 2014). Yet, few studies have rigorously examined these modalities in a combined, longitudinal RCT framework with neuroimaging endpoints.
By integrating these approaches, this study will address key questions:
Can multi-modal interventions reverse or stabilize biomarkers of neural plasticity in older adults?
How do improviment in neural plasticity correlate with measurable cognitive gains?
Are there age-dependent thresholds for intervention efficacy?
Aim 1: Establish baseline neural plasticity biomarkers across three aging cohorts (60–70, 70–80, 80+).

We will recruit 420 participants across three clinical research centers. Using multimodal neuroimaging (fMRI and EEG), we will idenfity age-related patterns of neural plasticity decline. Key biomarkers include resting-state functional connectivity, EEG coherence in fronto-parietal networks, and serum BDNF levels.
Older cohorts (80+) will exhibit significantly reduced plasticity markers, but maintain capacity for modulation through targeted interventions.
Aim 2: Evaluate the efficacy of a combined cognitive, exercise, and nutritional intervention protocol.
Participants will be randomly assigned to one of four groups:
Each participant will engage in the assigned protocol for 24 months, with periodic cognitive assessments and neuroimaging every six months.
The combined intervention will produce the largest effect size in neural connectivity, executive function, and memory outcomes.
Aim 3: Quantify longitudinal changes in neural and cognitive function and model mediating mechanisms.
We will employ advanced statistical modeling (latent growth curve, mixed-effects modeling) to examine causal relationships between behavioral adherence, biomarker changes, and cognitive improvements.

By 2050, the global population aged ≥ 65 years is expected to exceed 1.5 billion, with approximately 15–20 % experiencing measurable cognitive impairment (Prince et al., 2022). Age-related cognitive decline manifests as deterioration in executive function, working memory, and processing speed—domains underpinned by reduced neural connectivity and synaptic plasticity. Pharmacological interventions (e.g., cholinesterase inhibitors) have demonstrated limited efficacy, often providing temporary symptomatic relief without addressing underlying neuronal degeneration (Birks & Harvey, 2018).

Neural plasticity—the brain’s capacity to reorganize and form new connections—declines with age due to reduced neurogenesis, myelin degradation, and mitochondrial dysfunction (Burke & Barnes, 2020). Functional MRI studies show decreased resting-state connectivity in the default-mode and fronto-parietal networks, correlating with memory deficits (Andrews-Hanna et al., 2007). Yet, evidence suggests that neuroplasticity can be re-stimulated through enriched environments, aerobic activity, and cognitive challenge (Voss et al., 2013).
While numerous trials examine single interventions, few integrate

targeting complementary biological and cognitive systems. Meta-analyses indicate moderate improvements in cognition from exercise alone (Colcombe & Kramer, 2003) and cognitive training alone (Lampit et al., 2014), but their combined effect remains under-explored, particularly with neuroimaging validation. Thus, this study directly addresses a critical translational gap:
how synergistic behavioral interventions influence neural plasticity trajectories in older adults.
Our multidisciplinary team previously conducted a 12-month pilot (n = 60, ages 65–75) comparing cognitive training ± exercise. Preliminary results showed a
in the combined-intervention group relative to controls. Serum BDNF increased by ~ 18 %, supporting the hypothesized biological mediation (unpublished data, 2023).

Recruitment and retention exceeded expectations (dropout < 10 %). Participants demonstrated high adherence to digital cognitive training sessions (median 93 %), confirming logistical feasibility for the proposed R01 expansion across three clinical sites. Institutional MRI and EEG facilities have standardized acquisition pipelines, ensuring reproducibility and centralized quality control.
Each intervention modality acts on partially distinct, yet overlapping, mechanisms of neural plasticity:

ModalityPrimary MechanismKey Neural ImpactCognitive TrainingSynaptic strengthening, prefrontal efficiencyExecutive network integrationAerobic ExerciseAngiogenesis, BDNF up-regulationHippocampal volume, vascular integrityNutritional SupportAnti-inflammatory, membrane fluidityEnhanced neuronal repair and signaling
: coupling behavioral, physical, and nutritional stimuli may produce additive—or even multiplicative—effects on brain resilience (Ngandu et al., 2015)
The project is grounded in a
proposing that cognitive engagement, physical activity, and nutritional sufficiency collectively modulate synaptic signaling through three interconnected biological pathways:
– activity-dependent up-regulation of BDNF and IGF-1 supporting dendritic growth;
– aerobic exercise–induced angiogenesis enhancing oxygen and glucose delivery;
– nutrient-mediated reduction in oxidative stress preserving neuronal membranes.
within the hippocampal–prefrontal circuit, critical for memory and executive control.

Empirical models indicate that improvements in task-specific accuracy correlate with increased fMRI activation efficiency rather than gross activation volume (Lustig et al., 2009). The current framework posits that behavioral gains from training will map onto measurable neuroimaging signatures—namely, enhanced network efficiency and reduced intra-network noise.

, wherein the 60–70 cohort demonstrates the highest plastic potential, the 70–80 group shows attenuated yet significant improvement, and the 80 + group exhibits maintenance or slowed decline. Identifying this threshold is essential for developing age-tailored public-health interventions.
employing a 3 (age cohort) × 4 (intervention group) factorial design. Participants (N = 420) will be stratified by age and baseline cognitive status (normal vs mild impairment).
adaptive digital platform, 45 min × 3 sessions/week, targeting executive control, dual-tasking, and working-memory updating.
supervised treadmill or cycling, moderate intensity (60–70 % HRmax), 30 min × 5 sessions/week, monitored via wearable trackers.
daily administration of 1 g omega-3 EPA/DHA, 800 IU vitamin D3, and B-vitamin complex; compliance verified by plasma markers.
Single-blind design: outcome assessors unaware of group assignments. The nutrition and exercise arms will include matched placebo or light-stretch control activities to reduce expectancy bias.

Participants will be screened via medical history, cognitive evaluation, and physician clearance for exercise. Recruitment will occur through senior centers, community clinics, and aging-research registries at three collaborating institutions.

A Data Safety and Monitoring Board (DSMB) comprising neuroimaging, biostatistics, and gerontology experts will review progress semi-annually. Any adverse events will be reported within 48 hours per NIH guidelines. All code and analysis scripts will be version-controlled (GitHub Enterprise server) to ensure reproducibility.

Age-related cognitive decline affects approximately 15% of adults over 65, with neural plasticity markers showing consistent deterioration patterns across multiple brain regions. While current interventions focus primarily on pharmaceutical approaches, emerging evidence suggests multi-modal interventions combining cognitive training, physical exercise, and targeted nutritional supplementation may offer synergistic benefits.

This R01 proposal aims to (1) establish baseline neural plasticity biomarkers across three age cohorts (60–70, 70–80, 80+), (2) implement a randomized controlled trial of a novel multi-modal intervention protocol, and (3) quantify changes in both neural and cognitive function markers over a 24-month period. We hypothesize that combined intervention strategies will demonstrate superior outcomes compared to single-modality approaches.
We will recruit 420 participants across three sites, stratified by age and baseline cognitive function scores. The intervention combines:
(b) moderate-intensity aerobic exercise (5× weekly, 30 minutes), and
(c) targeted nutritional supplementation (Omega-3, B-vitamins, vitamin D).
Primary outcomes include fMRI-measured neural plasticity markers, EEG coherence patterns, and standardized cognitive assessments (MMSE, MoCA, custom executive function battery).

This study addresses a critical gap in understanding optimal intervention timing and composition for protecting cognitive function in aging populations. Results will inform clinical practice guidelines and establish evidence-based protocols for community-level implementation.

Effect sizes will be expressed as
Repeated-measures ANOVA to test differential responsiveness across age groups (60–70 vs. 70–80 vs. 80+).
Correlation and regression analyses linking intervention adherence to outcome magnitude (e.g., number of training sessions vs. MoCA gain).
Path analysis using structural equation modeling (SEM) to evaluate whether BDNF, IGF-1, or CRP changes mediate cognitive improvements.
The study will span two years and will proceed through
, each designed to ensure smooth execution, data integrity, and timely reporting.

During the initial phase, the research team will finalize the complete study protocol, secure institutional ethics approvals, and conduct staff training to ensure consistent methodology across all sites. Pilot testing for participant recruitment will also occur in this stage.
Institutional Review Board (IRB) approval and finalized Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).
This phase will involve screening and enrolling
across all research sites. Baseline data collection will include neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and biological sampling.
Completion of participant enrollment and a locked baseline dataset.

Participants will receive the multi-modal intervention protocol—comprising cognitive training, exercise routines, and nutritional supplementation—over a 12-month period. Mid-point assessments at 12 months will evaluate adherence and interim outcomes.
and a comprehensive mid-point progress report.
After completing the intervention, all participants will undergo final assessments, including neuroimaging, biomarker assays, and cognitive evaluations.
Completion of all endpoint assessments and a finalized dataset for analysis.
Participants receiving the combined cognitive, exercise, and nutritional interventions are expected to demonstrate significantly improved neural plasticity. This will manifest as increased
across frontal-parietal regions, and elevated serum
The intervention group is anticipated to show measurable gains in both executive function and memory, exceeding
relative to baseline scores. These improvements will be evident across multiple cognitive tests, including the Stroop, n-back, and Trail Making tasks.
Through integrated data analysis, the study aims to develop and validate a
that links participant adherence, biomarker dynamics, and cognitive performance changes. This model will serve as a tool for identifying key mediators of successful intervention outcomes.
A major deliverable of the project will be the creation of a
—comprising step-by-step guidelines for the scalable and sustainable implementation of the multi-modal intervention in community and healthcare settings.
High dropout rates could reduce statistical power and bias results. To minimize attrition, the study will employ
including telecoaching sessions, regular follow-ups, and gamified feedback mechanisms to maintain motivation and adherence.

Custom digital cognitive training platform with real-time adherence analytics.
Cross-site harmonization of imaging pipelines using containerized neuroinformatics infrastructure (Docker + BIDS-compliant).
Integration of wearable HR and sleep data streams for behavioral compliance verification.

, positing that optimal cognitive aging depends on multi-level modulation—neural, vascular, and biochemical. Findings will challenge reductionist models and establish a foundation for holistic neurogerontology frameworks.
If successful, this trial will redefine the standard of care for cognitive aging prevention by:
Reducing healthcare expenditures related to dementia and institutional care.
Empowering older adults through accessible self-management strategies (digital, exercise, diet-based).

Delaying cognitive decline by even 5 years could reduce dementia prevalence by ~40% (Brookmeyer et al., 2018). The scalable nature of our protocol offers immediate translational potential for health agencies, insurers, and senior-care providers.

This project will train 12 research assistants and 6 postdoctoral fellows across three sites, fostering interdisciplinary expertise in neuroscience, biostatistics, and aging research. Findings will be disseminated via:
Open-access data via NIH DASH repository
Workshops and webinars for clinicians and policymakers

Partnerships with local senior organizations and wellness centers will ensure equitable access and participant diversity. Engagement activities (educational talks, newsletters, online resources) will extend impact beyond academia.
